_comment: Worldview tree for the train to crazy town (metaphor coined by Ajeya Cotra, 80k #90). Nodes are GET-OFF POINTS (a fixed set of moral assumptions); edges are CRUCIAL-CONSIDERATION FLIPS (one step of moral expansion / one more bullet bitten). The decision rule never changes: every node ranks the SAME donation slate by expected welfare-adjusted DALYs averted per dollar. Only the assumptions change, so the winner ('top_pick') changes as you ride down. 'figures' names public, famous-ish people who most prominently ARTICULATE that stop's worldview - they are exemplars of the view, not necessarily people who 'get off' exactly there. This file is one canonical spine; BRANCHING is still TBD (see README).

nodes.desc: Least-crazy end of the line. Moral weight only on people near me; heavy pure-time discount; humans only; only near-certain, measurable effects count. Under these assumptions distant strangers, animals and the future are discounted to ~0, so the local/civic option wins. The pre-EA commonsense baseline; no single EA figure defends it.

Flip: drop the nationality discount (Peter Singer's famine argument is the origin). A life saved abroad counts like a life saved at home, so cheap, measurable global-health and cash interventions dominate. Alexander Berger is the prominent exemplar of deliberately getting off the train here and staying with robust, evidence-backed global health & wellbeing (GiveWell / Open Phil).

Flip: extend the moral circle to farmed vertebrates, weighted by a sentience-adjusted welfare range. Peter Singer (Animal Liberation) is the canonical articulator; Lewis Bollard leads Open Phil's farm-animal-welfare work. Corporate welfare campaigns avert enormous suffering per dollar and overtake human global health under most welfare-range estimates.

Flip: count invertebrates, with welfare range ~ (neuron count)^exponent. Brian Tomasik is the prominent voice on insect / invertebrate suffering. This is the worked example of the train's instability: sweeping the exponent from 0 to 2 swings shrimp welfare range across ~12 orders of magnitude and flips the ranking (edit neuron_exponent in the node's Squiggle model to watch the top pick move). It is the entry point for the soil-animal subgraph - macroarthropods / microarthropods / nematodes, each with its own conditions for whether welfare rises or falls.

Flip: stop discounting future lives. Toby Ord (The Precipice) and Will MacAskill (What We Owe The Future) are the prominent longtermists; Ajeya Cotra coined the 'train to crazy town' metaphor and rides roughly to here via worldview diversification, taking the future seriously while flagging where it gets uncomfortable. Reducing global-catastrophic risk (e.g. ALLFED's civilisational-resilience work) starts to dominate present-focused giving.

Last marked stop on this spine: accept Nick Bostrom's astronomical-waste argument and take small-probability / huge-payoff bets at face value; Eliezer Yudkowsky pushes AI-risk reasoning to its stark conclusions. Existential-risk reduction dominates everything. This is the far end Cotra's near-termist deliberately gets off before; ride further and you reach fanaticism / Pascal's-mugging territory.

_branching_TODO: This is one canonical spine. Real branching (accept/reject a consideration; multi-way settings of a parameter like the neuron-count exponent; alternative orderings) is undecided - see README 'Branching - TBD'. Nodes flagged subgraph:true are where a child tree opens once branching lands.
